parts.title: Part 1 — Community Sports Club enquiry
parts.scenario: A conversation between a new resident and the membership officer at the Meadowbank Community Sports Club.
parts.transcript:
Officer: Good afternoon, Meadowbank Sports Club, this is Daniel. How can I help?
Caller: Hi, I've just moved to the area and I'd like to join the club.
Officer: Wonderful. Let me take a few details. Can I have your full name?
Caller: It's Rachel Simmons. That's S-I-M-M-O-N-S.
Officer: Thank you, Rachel. And what's the best contact number?
Caller: It's oh-seven-nine-double-four, three-one-two, eight-oh-six.
Officer: Great. Now, we have three membership levels. The Standard membership is thirty-two pounds a month and covers the gym and swimming pool. The Premium is forty-eight pounds and adds all the fitness classes. And there's a Concession rate of twenty pounds for students and over-sixties.
Caller: I'm a full-time student, so the Concession sounds right.
Officer: Perfect. You'll need to bring proof of enrolment. Now, which activities interest you most?
Caller: Mainly swimming, and I'd love to try the badminton.
Officer: The pool is open from six in the morning until ten at night. Badminton courts must be booked in advance — they're very popular on Tuesday evenings.
Caller: Good to know. How do I find you? I'll be coming from the train station.
Officer: From the station, walk down King Street, and we're the large brick building next to the public library.
Caller: Next to the library, got it. And when could I come in to register?
Officer: We do inductions on Thursdays at four o'clock. Ask for the duty manager, Priya.
Caller: Thursday at four, ask for Priya. Thanks so much, Daniel.

questions.question: Caller's surname:
questions.explanation: She spells it out: 'S-I-M-M-O-N-S'.

questions.question: Contact number ends in the digits:
questions.explanation: '...three-one-two, eight-oh-six' — the number ends 806.

questions.question: How much is the Standard membership per month?
questions.explanation: 'The Standard membership is thirty-two pounds a month'.

questions.question: Which membership does the caller choose?
questions.explanation: As a full-time student she says 'the Concession sounds right'.

questions.question: To get the concession rate, the caller must bring proof of ______.
questions.explanation: 'You'll need to bring proof of enrolment'.

questions.question: What time does the swimming pool close?
questions.explanation: 'The pool is open from six in the morning until ten at night'.

questions.question: Badminton courts are busiest on which evening?
questions.explanation: 'they're very popular on Tuesday evenings'.

questions.question: The club is located next to the:
questions.explanation: 'we're the large brick building next to the public library'.

questions.question: Inductions take place on which day?
questions.explanation: 'We do inductions on Thursdays at four o'clock'.

questions.question: At registration, the caller should ask for the duty manager named:
questions.explanation: 'Ask for the duty manager, Priya'.

parts.title: Part 2 — Guided tour of the Riverside Regeneration Project
parts.scenario: A monologue in which a guide describes the layout and facilities of a redeveloped riverside district.
parts.transcript: Welcome to the Riverside Regeneration Project. Let me orient you before we walk. We're standing at the main entrance on the south side. Directly ahead of you, across the central plaza, is the old grain warehouse, which has been converted into an art gallery — that's the tall building with the glass roof. To your left, running along the water, is the promenade, lined with cafés and small independent shops. To your right, you'll see the children's play area, and just beyond it, the community garden that local residents planted last spring. Now, the project has three phases. Phase one, completed two years ago, focused on the housing you can see to the north. Phase two, which finished in March, delivered the office spaces along the eastern edge. Phase three is still under construction and will add a cinema and a covered market by next winter. A few practical notes: the visitor car park is free at weekends but charges apply on weekdays. There are toilets next to the gallery entrance. If you need refreshments, the café at the end of the promenade has the best views but tends to be busy; the kiosk by the play area is quieter. Please stay on the marked paths near the construction zone, and note that the footbridge to the north bank is closed until the end of the month for repairs. We'll begin our walk at the gallery, then follow the promenade, and finish at the community garden, where there's a small exhibition about the history of the site.

questions.question: The old grain warehouse is now used as a(n):
questions.explanation: 'the old grain warehouse, which has been converted into an art gallery'.

questions.question: The promenade with cafés and shops runs along the:
questions.explanation: 'To your left, running along the water, is the promenade, lined with cafés and small independent shops'.

questions.question: What was planted by local residents?
questions.explanation: 'the community garden that local residents planted last spring'.

questions.question: Phase one delivered the:
questions.explanation: 'Phase one... focused on the housing you can see to the north'.

questions.question: In which month did phase two finish?
questions.explanation: 'Phase two, which finished in March, delivered the office spaces'.

questions.question: Phase three will add a covered market and a:
questions.explanation: 'Phase three... will add a cinema and a covered market by next winter'.

questions.question: The visitor car park is free:
questions.explanation: 'the visitor car park is free at weekends but charges apply on weekdays'.

questions.question: Which café tends to be busy but has the best views?
options: the kiosk by the play area
the café at the end of the promenade
questions.answer: the café at the end of the promenade
questions.explanation: 'the café at the end of the promenade has the best views but tends to be busy'.

questions.question: The footbridge to the north bank is closed for:
questions.explanation: 'the footbridge to the north bank is closed until the end of the month for repairs'.

questions.question: The tour will finish at the:
questions.explanation: 'finish at the community garden, where there's a small exhibition'.

parts.title: Part 3 — Students discussing an urban planning assignment
parts.scenario: Two students, Mia and Tom, talk with their tutor about a project on car-free city centres.
parts.transcript:
Tutor: So, Mia, Tom — how's the project on car-free city centres coming along?
Mia: Quite well. We've been comparing three cities that pedestrianised their centres. The main challenge has been finding reliable data on air quality before and after.
Tom: Yeah, the traffic figures were easy to get, but the pollution measurements were inconsistent between cities.
Tutor: That's a common problem. What did you find about the economic effects?
Mia: That surprised us. We expected shops to lose money when cars were banned, but in two of the three cities, footfall actually increased and retail revenue went up.
Tom: The exception was the third city, where a lot of shops complained. But we think that's because they didn't provide enough public transport as an alternative.
Tutor: A fair point. Access matters. Now, for the presentation, how are you dividing the work?
Mia: I'll cover the environmental data, and Tom's doing the economic analysis. We still need someone to handle the social impact section.
Tutor: I'd suggest you both contribute to that, rather than leaving it to one person — it ties the other two parts together.
Tom: Makes sense. One thing we're unsure about is the conclusion. Should we recommend car-free centres for all cities?
Tutor: I'd be cautious. Your own data shows it depends on local conditions — especially transport links. A blanket recommendation would overstate your evidence.
Mia: Right, so we'll frame it as conditional rather than universal.
Tutor: Exactly. And don't forget to cite the original studies, not just the news articles that summarise them.

questions.question: How many cities are Mia and Tom comparing?
questions.explanation: 'We've been comparing three cities that pedestrianised their centres'.

questions.question: What has been the main challenge in their research?
questions.explanation: 'The main challenge has been finding reliable data on air quality'.

questions.question: In two of the three cities, retail revenue:
questions.explanation: 'footfall actually increased and retail revenue went up'.

questions.question: Why did shops struggle in the third city?
options: not enough public transport was provided
questions.answer: not enough public transport was provided
questions.explanation: 'they didn't provide enough public transport as an alternative'.

questions.question: Which section will Mia cover?
questions.explanation: 'I'll cover the environmental data, and Tom's doing the economic analysis'.

questions.question: The tutor suggests that the social impact section should be handled by:
questions.explanation: 'I'd suggest you both contribute to that, rather than leaving it to one person'.

questions.question: The students are unsure about how to write the:
questions.explanation: 'One thing we're unsure about is the conclusion'.

questions.question: The tutor advises framing the recommendation as:
questions.explanation: 'we'll frame it as conditional rather than universal' — agreeing with the tutor's caution.

questions.question: According to the tutor, a blanket recommendation would ______ the evidence.
questions.explanation: 'A blanket recommendation would overstate your evidence'.

questions.question: The tutor reminds them to cite the original studies rather than:
questions.explanation: 'cite the original studies, not just the news articles that summarise them'.

parts.title: Part 4 — Lecture on the history of urban parks
parts.scenario: A university lecturer discusses how public parks developed in industrial cities.
parts.transcript: Today I want to trace how the public park became a fixture of the modern city. Before the nineteenth century, most green spaces in cities were private — the gardens of the wealthy or royal hunting grounds occasionally opened to a select public. The turning point came with rapid industrialisation. As factory towns swelled, reformers grew alarmed at the lack of fresh air and open space for ordinary workers. They argued that parks were not a luxury but a matter of public health. The first park designed specifically for the general public opened in Birkenhead, near Liverpool, in 1847. Crucially, it was funded by the local ratepayers and owned by the town, establishing the principle that a park was public property. Its designer, Joseph Paxton, created a landscape of winding paths, lakes, and carefully framed views — a deliberate contrast to the grid of the surrounding streets. Birkenhead proved hugely influential. When the American designer Frederick Law Olmsted visited, he was so impressed that he drew on its ideas for New York's Central Park. Over the following decades, parks spread across industrial cities, but their purpose was debated. Some saw them purely as places for quiet contemplation and discouraged games and gatherings. Others insisted parks should host active recreation — sports pitches, bandstands, and boating lakes. By the early twentieth century, the second view had largely won, and parks became lively social spaces. One lasting legacy is the idea that access to green space is a right of citizenship, not a privilege — an idea that still shapes planning debates today, as cities compete for land and green space comes under pressure from development.

questions.question: Before the nineteenth century, most urban green spaces were:
questions.explanation: 'most green spaces in cities were private — the gardens of the wealthy or royal hunting grounds'.

questions.question: Reformers argued that parks were a matter of public ______.
questions.explanation: 'They argued that parks were not a luxury but a matter of public health'.

questions.question: The first park for the general public opened in Birkenhead in which year?
questions.explanation: 'The first park designed specifically for the general public opened in Birkenhead... in 1847'.

questions.question: The park was funded by the local:
questions.explanation: 'it was funded by the local ratepayers and owned by the town'.

questions.question: Who designed the Birkenhead park?
questions.explanation: 'Its designer, Joseph Paxton, created a landscape of winding paths'.

questions.question: Paxton's design was a deliberate contrast to the surrounding:
questions.explanation: 'a deliberate contrast to the grid of the surrounding streets'.

questions.question: Which American park was influenced by Birkenhead?
questions.explanation: 'he drew on its ideas for New York's Central Park'.

questions.question: The designer who visited from America was:
questions.explanation: 'the American designer Frederick Law Olmsted visited'.

questions.question: By the early twentieth century, parks were mainly seen as places for:
questions.explanation: 'the second view had largely won, and parks became lively social spaces'.

questions.question: The lecturer says a lasting legacy is that green space is seen as a right of ______.
questions.explanation: 'access to green space is a right of citizenship, not a privilege'.

passages.title: The Return of the Tram
passages.passage:
For much of the twentieth century, the electric tram seemed a relic of a bygone age. Cities across Europe and North America tore up their tram lines in the decades after the Second World War, convinced that the future belonged to the private car and the diesel bus. Trams were dismissed as slow, inflexible, and an obstacle to the free flow of motor traffic. By the 1960s, hundreds of tram systems had been dismantled, their rails paved over and their overhead wires removed.

Yet within a generation, the tram staged a remarkable comeback. The turning point came as cities confronted the consequences of car dependency: choking congestion, worsening air quality, and sprawling development that made public transport ever harder to sustain. Planners began to look again at the tram, and to appreciate qualities they had earlier overlooked. Unlike buses, trams run on fixed rails, which signals a permanent commitment to a route. This permanence, research suggests, encourages investment: developers are far more willing to build near a tram line than a bus route, because the rails cannot simply be rerouted or withdrawn.

Modern trams also address the old complaints. Running on dedicated tracks separated from other traffic, they are no longer trapped behind cars. They are quiet, produce no local emissions, and can carry far more passengers than a bus. In cities from Bordeaux to Manchester, new tram systems have proved strikingly popular, often exceeding their projected passenger numbers within a few years of opening.

Critics point out that trams are expensive to build. Laying track and stringing overhead wires costs far more per kilometre than painting a bus lane. There is truth in this, but supporters argue that the comparison is misleading. A tram system, once built, lasts for decades and its running costs are low. Moreover, the economic benefits of the development it attracts can far outweigh the initial outlay. The French city of Bordeaux, whose tram network opened in 2003, is frequently cited as a case where the tram catalysed the regeneration of a tired city centre.

Not every scheme has succeeded, of course. Where trams have been added without restricting cars, or where routes have been poorly chosen, the results have been disappointing. The lesson that planners have drawn is that a tram is not a magic solution in itself, but part of a wider strategy — one that must include measures to discourage car use if the full benefits are to be realised. The tram, in short, has returned not as a piece of nostalgia, but as a considered response to the problems that its abolition helped to create.

questions.question: In the decades after the Second World War, many cities removed their tram lines.
questions.explanation: 'Cities across Europe and North America tore up their tram lines in the decades after the Second World War'.

questions.question: Trams were more popular than buses in the 1960s.
questions.explanation: The passage says systems were dismantled but makes no comparison of tram vs bus popularity in the 1960s.

questions.question: According to the passage, developers prefer building near tram lines because the rails represent a permanent route.
questions.explanation: 'developers are far more willing to build near a tram line than a bus route, because the rails cannot simply be rerouted or withdrawn'.

questions.question: Modern trams produce more local emissions than buses.
questions.explanation: 'They are quiet, produce no local emissions' — the opposite of the statement.

questions.question: What do modern trams run on that keeps them out of other traffic?
questions.explanation: 'Running on dedicated tracks separated from other traffic, they are no longer trapped behind cars'.

questions.question: In which year did the Bordeaux tram network open?
questions.explanation: 'The French city of Bordeaux, whose tram network opened in 2003'.

questions.question: Compared with a bus lane, building a tram line per kilometre is:
questions.explanation: 'Laying track and stringing overhead wires costs far more per kilometre than painting a bus lane'.

questions.question: Supporters argue that the cost comparison with buses is misleading because trams:
options: last for decades with low running costs
questions.answer: last for decades with low running costs
questions.explanation: 'A tram system, once built, lasts for decades and its running costs are low'.

questions.question: Which factor does the passage identify as necessary for a tram scheme to deliver its full benefits?
questions.explanation: 'part of a wider strategy — one that must include measures to discourage car use'.

questions.question: New tram systems in cities like Bordeaux and Manchester often carried ______ passengers than projected.
questions.explanation: 'often exceeding their projected passenger numbers within a few years of opening'.

questions.question: Trams disappointed where they were introduced without restricting cars.
questions.explanation: 'Where trams have been added without restricting cars... the results have been disappointing'.

questions.question: The passage concludes that the tram has returned mainly as a piece of nostalgia.
questions.explanation: 'the tram has returned not as a piece of nostalgia, but as a considered response to the problems'.

questions.question: What quality of trams, absent in buses, signals a permanent commitment to a route?
questions.explanation: 'trams run on fixed rails, which signals a permanent commitment to a route'.

passages.title: How Cities Keep Their Cool
passages.passage:
A. Anyone who has crossed a sun-baked car park on a summer afternoon has felt the phenomenon that scientists call the urban heat island. Cities are consistently warmer than the countryside that surrounds them, sometimes by as much as several degrees. On a still summer night the difference can be dramatic, and it is growing more serious as the climate warms and urban populations expand.

B. The causes are well understood. Concrete, brick, and asphalt absorb the sun's heat during the day and release it slowly at night, so cities never fully cool down. Dark surfaces such as roads and roofs absorb far more heat than the pale, reflective surfaces they replaced. Meanwhile, the removal of vegetation eliminates the natural cooling that plants provide, and the waste heat from vehicles, air conditioners, and industry adds still more warmth to the urban air.

C. The consequences are not merely a matter of comfort. Heatwaves kill, and they kill disproportionately in cities, where the heat island effect pushes night-time temperatures above the level at which the human body can recover from the day's heat. The elderly, the very young, and those without air conditioning are most at risk. As heatwaves become more frequent and intense, planners are searching for ways to bring urban temperatures down.

D. One of the simplest measures is also one of the oldest: planting trees. A mature tree cools the air both by providing shade and by releasing water vapour through its leaves, a process known as transpiration. Studies have found that a well-shaded street can be several degrees cooler than a bare one. Trees are not a complete answer — they take years to grow, require maintenance, and compete with pipes and cables for underground space — but few interventions offer so much benefit for so little cost.

E. A newer approach targets the roofs and walls that make up so much of a city's surface. Painting roofs white, or covering them with reflective coatings, can dramatically reduce the heat a building absorbs. So-called green roofs, planted with vegetation, go further, adding insulation and absorbing rainwater as well as cooling the air. Some cities now require reflective or green roofs on new buildings, and the results, though modest for any single structure, add up across a whole district.

F. Water, too, has a role. Fountains, ponds, and canals cool the air as water evaporates from their surface, which is why historic cities in hot climates were often built around watercourses. Modern designers are rediscovering this principle, incorporating water features not merely for decoration but as a deliberate cooling strategy. Even temporary measures, such as spraying water in public squares during heatwaves, can bring noticeable relief.

G. No single measure will tame the urban heat island. But taken together — more trees, cooler roofs, more water, and a general reduction in the paved surfaces that trap heat — they can make a real difference. The challenge for planners is to weave these elements into cities that were, for the most part, designed with no thought for the heat they would one day have to endure.

questions.question: Choose the best heading for Paragraph B:
questions.explanation: Paragraph B lists the causes: heat-absorbing surfaces, loss of vegetation, and waste heat.

questions.question: Choose the best heading for Paragraph C:
questions.explanation: Paragraph C is about heatwaves killing people, especially in cities.

questions.question: Choose the best heading for Paragraph D:
questions.explanation: Paragraph D is entirely about planting trees and their cooling effect.

questions.question: Choose the best heading for Paragraph E:
questions.explanation: Paragraph E covers white/reflective roofs and green roofs.

questions.question: By how much can cities be warmer than surrounding countryside?

by as much as several degrees
questions.answer: by as much as several degrees
questions.explanation: 'Cities are consistently warmer than the countryside... sometimes by as much as several degrees'.

questions.question: Dark surfaces absorb more heat than the pale surfaces they replaced.
questions.explanation: 'Dark surfaces such as roads and roofs absorb far more heat than the pale, reflective surfaces they replaced'.

questions.explanation: 'the heat island effect pushes night-time temperatures above the level at which the human body can recover'.

questions.explanation: 'releasing water vapour through its leaves, a process known as transpiration'.

questions.explanation: 'Trees are not a complete answer — they take years to grow, require maintenance...'.

questions.question: Green roofs also help by absorbing:
questions.explanation: 'green roofs... adding insulation and absorbing rainwater as well as cooling the air'.

questions.question: Historic cities in hot climates were often built around:
questions.explanation: 'historic cities in hot climates were often built around watercourses'.

questions.question: Spraying water in public squares during heatwaves can provide relief.
questions.explanation: 'spraying water in public squares during heatwaves, can bring noticeable relief'.

questions.question: The passage's overall argument is that urban heat is best tackled by:
questions.explanation: 'No single measure will tame the urban heat island. But taken together... they can make a real difference'.

passages.title: The Fifteen-Minute City
passages.passage:
In recent years, an idea with a catchy name has captured the imagination of urban planners: the fifteen-minute city. The concept, popularised by the French-Colombian academic Carlos Moreno, is deceptively simple. In a fifteen-minute city, residents can meet most of their daily needs — work, shopping, education, healthcare, and leisure — within a short walk or bicycle ride of their homes. The car, if it is needed at all, becomes an occasional convenience rather than a daily necessity.

The appeal is easy to grasp. Cities built around the car have spread outwards, separating the places where people live from the places where they work, shop, and play. The result is longer journeys, more traffic, and neighbourhoods that empty out during the working day. Proponents of the fifteen-minute city argue that bringing daily destinations closer together would cut car use, reduce emissions, revive local high streets, and foster a stronger sense of community.

The idea is not, in fact, new. For most of human history, cities were necessarily compact, because people travelled on foot. What is new is the deliberate effort to recreate that proximity in cities that have grown used to the car. Paris has been among the most enthusiastic adopters, expanding cycle lanes, converting road space to other uses, and encouraging local services in every neighbourhood. Other cities, from Melbourne to Portland, have pursued versions of the same goal, sometimes under different names.

Implementing the concept is harder than describing it. Existing cities cannot simply be redrawn; their buildings, roads, and property boundaries are largely fixed. Introducing local shops and workplaces into a residential district may require changes to planning rules that have long kept these functions apart. There is also the risk that making a neighbourhood more attractive drives up property prices, pushing out the very residents the changes were meant to help — a process familiar to anyone who has watched a once-affordable district become fashionable.

The concept has also, unexpectedly, become a target of suspicion. A small but vocal group has claimed that fifteen-minute cities are a plot to confine people to their neighbourhoods, misreading a proposal about access as one about restriction. Planners have been at pains to point out that the aim is to give people more choices close to home, not to prevent them from travelling further if they wish. The confusion illustrates how easily a technical idea about urban design can be swept up into wider anxieties.

Whatever its difficulties, the fifteen-minute city has succeeded in reframing a question that had long gone unasked: not how to move people quickly across a city, but how to arrange a city so that they need to move less. That shift in perspective may prove more durable than the slogan itself. Even planners sceptical of the label concede that reducing the distances of everyday life is a sensible goal — one that cities pursued instinctively for centuries before the car persuaded them to forget it.

questions.question: Who is credited with popularising the fifteen-minute city concept?
questions.explanation: 'The concept, popularised by the French-Colombian academic Carlos Moreno'.

questions.question: In a fifteen-minute city, the car becomes:
questions.explanation: 'The car, if it is needed at all, becomes an occasional convenience rather than a daily necessity'.

questions.question: According to the passage, car-centred cities have caused neighbourhoods to empty out during the working day.
questions.explanation: 'neighbourhoods that empty out during the working day'.

questions.question: The passage says the fifteen-minute city is a completely new idea.
questions.explanation: 'The idea is not, in fact, new. For most of human history, cities were necessarily compact'.

questions.question: Which city is described as among the most enthusiastic adopters?
questions.explanation: 'Paris has been among the most enthusiastic adopters, expanding cycle lanes'.

questions.question: One difficulty of implementing the concept is that existing cities have largely fixed buildings, roads, and property ______.
questions.explanation: 'their buildings, roads, and property boundaries are largely fixed'.

questions.question: Making a neighbourhood more attractive may push up:
questions.explanation: 'making a neighbourhood more attractive drives up property prices, pushing out the very residents the changes were meant to help'.

questions.question: Critics who suspect the concept have interpreted a proposal about access as one about:
questions.explanation: 'misreading a proposal about access as one about restriction'.

questions.question: Planners say the aim of the fifteen-minute city is to give people more choices close to home.
questions.explanation: 'the aim is to give people more choices close to home, not to prevent them from travelling further'.

questions.question: The fifteen-minute city has been unanimously welcomed by the public.
questions.explanation: 'The concept has also, unexpectedly, become a target of suspicion' from a vocal group.

questions.question: The passage says the concept reframes the key planning question as how to arrange a city so people need to move ______.
questions.explanation: 'how to arrange a city so that they need to move less'.

questions.question: Choose the best heading for the final paragraph:
questions.explanation: The final paragraph argues the change in perspective 'may prove more durable than the slogan itself'.

questions.question: Even sceptical planners concede that reducing the distances of everyday life is a sensible goal.
questions.explanation: 'Even planners sceptical of the label concede that reducing the distances of everyday life is a sensible goal'.

questions.question: What persuaded cities to 'forget' the goal of short everyday distances?
questions.explanation: 'cities pursued instinctively for centuries before the car persuaded them to forget it'.

task1.prompt: The chart below shows the percentage of commuters using different modes of transport to travel to work in a European city in 2005 and 2020. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.

chartData.title: Modes of transport used to commute to work (2005 vs 2020)

task2.prompt: In many cities, private cars are being restricted in favour of public transport, cycling, and walking. Some people believe this improves quality of life, while others argue it causes inconvenience and harms businesses. Discuss both views and give your own opinion. Give reasons for your answer and include any relevant examples from your own knowledge or experience. Write at least 250 words.

questions: Let's talk about where you live. Do you live in a city, a town, or the countryside?
What do you like most about the area you live in?
Is there anything you would like to change about your neighbourhood?
How do you usually travel around your local area?
Do you think your area is a good place for young people to live? Why?

part2.cueCard: Describe a public place in your city that you find useful or enjoyable. You should say: what the place is and where it is located; how often you go there and who you go with; what you do there; and explain why you find this place useful or enjoyable.
points: Preparation note: you have one minute to make notes before speaking for up to two minutes.
What the place is and where it is located
How often you go there and who with
What you do there and why you value it

questions: Why do you think public spaces such as parks and squares are important for a city?
How have public spaces in your country changed over the last few decades?
Some people say cities are becoming less friendly places to live. Do you agree?
What responsibilities do city governments have in providing public spaces?
Do you think technology will change the way people use public spaces in the future?
